nativeRange.habitat: Short Tentacle Plate Corals are found on reef slopes, sandy lagoon floors, and rubble zones from shallow water down to around 30 metres. Unlike most stony corals, adults are free-living and rest unattached on soft substrate rather than cementing to rock.

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Benefits from supplemental target feeding of mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty marine foods, and zooplankton once or twice a week when tentacles are extended at night. Has been documented accepting sinking coral pellets. Feeding is not strictly required if lighting and water quality are adequate.

goodWith: other LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals placed in direct contact
other plate corals without adequate spacing

imageCandidates.notes: Fungia fungites. Photo (c) Attila Olah. ~2048x1152. In-situ free-living plate coral, tack-sharp, whole oval disc with vivid pink/cream radial coloration - the showiest, most representative Fungia plate.
Fungia fungites. Photo (c) Greg Tasney. ~2048x1250. Sharp in-situ shot of a vivid magenta/purple morph showing radial septa; illustrates the purple colour variant.
Fungia fungites. Photo (c) Mike Burnett. ~2048x1536. Whole disc on rubble showing green/pink bicolour morph; sharp, good top-down framing.
Lobactis scutaria (former Fungia scutaria). Photo (c) Thomas Irvine. ~2048x1536. In-situ with tentacles extended showing fluorescent green tips; sharp, distinct species.
Lobactis scutaria (former Fungia scutaria). Photo (c) Robin White. ~1368x2048. Whole cream/tan elongate disc on colourful reef; sharp, small corner watermark.
Pleuractis granulosa (former Fungia granulosa). Photo (c) Debra Baker. ~1536x2048. Whole oval disc on sand, sharp; tan/orange colour, distinct species.
Pleuractis paumotensis. Photo (c) Debra Baker. ~2048x1536. Golden-brown oval free-living plate on sand; sharp, whole animal, distinct species.
Pleuractis paumotensis. Photo (c) Debra Baker. ~1536x2048. Green elongate disc in-situ with clear central mouth line; sharp.
Danafungia horrida (former Fungia horrida). Photo (c) Debra Baker. ~2048x1536. Round green plate coral in-situ; whole disc, distinct species.
Danafungia scruposa (former Fungia scruposa). Photo (c) Debra Baker. ~2048x1536. Cream/yellow round disc, sharp radial septa, whole animal.
Danafungia scruposa (former Fungia scruposa). Photo (c) Debra Baker. ~2048x1536. Two free-living green plate corals together, showing the solitary disc form in habitat.
Fungia valida (Pleuractis valida). Photo by Nick Hobgood. 2774x2076. Free-living plate with tentacles extended over sand; sharp, whole animal, adds another species.

summary: The Short Tentacle Plate Coral is a free-living, solitary large polyp stony coral from the Indo-Pacific. It rests on the sandbed, is capable of slow self-propulsion, and is generally considered beginner-friendly, needing moderate light and low to medium indirect flow.
careNotes:
Taxonomic note: The genus Fungia was substantially revised through molecular phylogenetics. The most commonly traded aquarium species, long sold as Fungia repanda, is now accepted under the name Lithophyllon repanda (Dana, 1846) in major scientific databases; the older name Fungia sp. remains widespread in the aquarium trade.

Key care points:
• Placement: Place on a flat, open area of the sandbed. This coral is capable of slow self-propulsion — it has been reported to move across the substrate to find better light or avoid being smothered by sediment. It will also inflate with water to roughly double its resting size, a normal behavior for light-seeking and sand-clearing. Ensure the surrounding area has at least 15–20 cm of clearance on all sides.
• Lighting: Moderate lighting is generally recommended, widely cited as 75–200 PAR, with many keepers reporting good results in the 80–150 PAR range. Acclimate to the target lighting level gradually to avoid bleaching.
• Flow: Low to moderate, indirect flow is preferred. Direct high-velocity flow can damage the fleshy polyp tissue and prevent full inflation.
• Feeding: Although the Short Tentacle Plate Coral is photosynthetic through its zooxanthellae (symbiotic algae living within its tissue), supplemental target feeding tends to improve growth and coloration. Offer small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or finely minced krill — once or twice a week when the tentacles are extended, typically at night or in low light.
• Aggression: Despite its short tentacles, this coral can damage adjacent corals on contact and has been noted to emit tissue-damaging mucus. Spacing from neighboring corals is important, particularly since the coral may slowly relocate toward them over time.
• Calcium and alkalinity: Maintain calcium around 400–450 ppm, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, and magnesium 1250–1350 ppm for healthy calcification.
• Care level: Most sources characterize this as an easy coral well-suited to beginner reef keepers, though at least one specialist source rates it as more challenging due to difficulties in long-term maintenance and the limited availability of aquacultured specimens.
• Color variants: Available in green, orange, pink, purple, red, and blue morphs — all share identical husbandry requirements.
• Distinction from Long Tentacle Plate Coral: The closely related species sold as Long Tentacle Plate Coral (Heliofungia sp.) has distinctly longer, more anemone-like tentacles and different care needs. Fungia and Heliofungia are separate genera despite their similar disc-shaped form.
breedingNotes: Not reproduced in captivity through sexual means. Fragging is not typically practiced due to the coral's free-living single-polyp structure. Wild populations reproduce sexually via broadcast spawning. Juveniles begin life attached to hard substrate and become free-living upon maturity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Short Tentacle Plate Coral
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed scientific name Fungia repanda (=Lithophyllon repanda), care level Easy, temperament Semi-Aggressive, placement Bottom, flow Low to Medium, lighting Moderate, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12. Feeding: a few times per week with mysis/brine/zooplankton.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Fungia Plate Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temp 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), SG 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, lighting 80–150 PAR, low to moderate indirect flow. Care level beginner-intermediate. Feeding 1–2x/week. Temperament generally peaceful with aggression potential on contact. Averaged into final parameters.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Fungia Plate Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temp 77–84°F (25–28.9°C), alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, lighting 150–200 PAR, flow low-moderate, placement bottom. Rated care level ADVANCED and temperament AGGRESSIVE — outlier vs other sources. Upper temperature and PAR values from this source contributed to averages. Care level set to beginner per majority consensus; aggression note included in careNotes.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Plate Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed calcium ~425 ppm, magnesium ~1350 ppm, alkalinity ~8 dKH, lighting 30–150 PAR range (medium to low), flow medium to low. Noted self-propulsion behavior. Care level described as easy.

additional.name: Corals of the World – Fungia repanda
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy and adult size confirmation. Adult size: circular polyp up to 300 mm (30 cm) diameter. Habitat: reef slopes and lagoons. Confirmed family Fungiidae, order Scleractinia.
